methodology_sections: 2.3 Data Flow Tracing - Input flow, external data, asset movement, permission escalation
3.2 Business Logic Analysis - Economic incentives, edge cases, assumption violations, race conditions

data_flows.source: msg.sender (buyer) sends ETH via buyCurvesToken / buyCurvesTokenWithName / buyCurvesTokenForPresale / buyCurvesTokenWhitelisted
path: buyCurvesToken validates presale timing
_buyCurvesToken validates supply > 0 or msg.sender == subject
getPrice computes bonding curve price
getFees computes fee breakdown
Require msg.value >= price + totalFee
Update curvesTokenBalance and curvesTokenSupply
_transferFees distributes fees via .call
feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange resets buyer offset (LOSES accumulated fees)
feeRedistributor.addFees distributes holder fee
_addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject tracks ownership
transformations: supply → bonding curve price via quadratic sum formula
price → fee amounts via percentage multiplication / 1 ether
sinks: protocolFeeDestination receives protocolFee (+ referralFee if no referral)
Curves contract retains price portion of ETH
EXCESS ETH from overpayment stays LOCKED in contract (never refunded)
data_flows.trust_changes: User ETH → contract custody; external .call transfers give execution to arbitrary addresses
validation_points: Presale: startTime check, merkle proof, maxBuy check
exposure_risks: Reentrancy via .call to protocolFeeDestination, curvesTokenSubject, or referralFeeDestination
Front-running: attacker can see pending buy and buy first to increase price
manipulation_risks: Excess ETH never refunded — MEV bots/users lose overpayments permanently
Anyone can set fee parameters due to broken Security.sol modifiers
getPrice reverts for supply=0 and amount>1, limiting initial buy to 1 token

path: Validate supply > amount (last token cannot be sold)
Validate curvesTokenBalance >= amount
getPrice computes sell price at (supply - amount, amount)
Decrease curvesTokenBalance and curvesTokenSupply
_transferFees for sell: compute fees, distribute
transformations: supply - amount → price via bonding curve
sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee
sinks: msg.sender receives sellValue (price minus ALL fees)
CRITICAL: protocolFee stays in contract — NEVER sent to protocolFeeDestination
CRITICAL: referralFee stays in contract if no referral defined
exposure_risks: Reentrancy after state update — msg.sender receives ETH first, could re-enter
manipulation_risks: Protocol fee on sells is permanently locked in contract, never reaches protocolFeeDestination
Referral fee on sells locked if no referral set — seller loses this fee amount
onBalanceChange called for seller resets offset without crediting accumulated fees — seller loses unclaimed holder fees

data_flows.source: msg.sender calls withdraw(curvesTokenSubject, amount) or deposit(curvesTokenSubject, amount)
path: Withdraw: validate balance, deploy ERC20 if needed, _transfer internal tokens to contract, mint ERC20 tokens
Deposit: validate amount % 1 ether == 0, validate ERC20 balance, burn ERC20 tokens, _transfer internal tokens from contract to user
transformations: Withdraw: internal balance (0 decimals) → ERC20 (18 decimals) via amount * 1 ether
Deposit: ERC20 amount (18 decimals) → internal balance (0 decimals) via amount / 1 ether
sinks: Withdraw: ERC20 tokens minted to msg.sender, internal tokens held by Curves contract
Deposit: ERC20 tokens burned, internal tokens transferred to msg.sender
data_flows.trust_changes: Internal token custody changes between user and Curves contract; ERC20 representation created/destroyed
validation_points: Deposit: amount % 1 ether == 0, ERC20 balance check, contract internal balance check
exposure_risks: Withdraw bypasses onlyTokenSubject for ERC20 deployment — any holder can trigger deployment with default name
No FeeSplitter notification (onBalanceChange not called) — fee distribution becomes inaccurate
manipulation_risks: CRITICAL: Withdraw/deposit cycle can steal holder fees — user withdraws (balance drops, offset unchanged), fees distributed to fewer holders at higher rate, user deposits back (balance restored), claims fees at full balance retroactively covering the high-rate period
FeeSplitter totalSupply changes on withdraw/deposit but no offset updates — fee accounting desynchronized

data_flows.source: Holder fees from _transferFees in Curves → FeeSplitter.addFees
path: Curves._transferFees calls feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange(subject, buyer)
onBalanceChange resets buyer's userFeeOffset to cumulativeFeePerToken (WITHOUT crediting existing owed fees)
onBalanceChange pushes token to userTokens (even duplicates)
Curves._transferFees calls feeRedistributor.addFees with holderFee ETH
addFees increments cumulativeFeePerToken by msg.value * PRECISION / totalSupply_
transformations: ETH fee → cumulative per-token fee tracker
sinks: User claims via claimFees or batchClaiming → ETH transferred via .transfer()
data_flows.trust_changes: ETH moves from Curves → FeeSplitter → individual claimants
exposure_risks: payable(msg.sender).transfer() limited to 2300 gas — fails for smart contract wallets
batchClaiming could run out of gas with large tokenList
manipulation_risks: onBalanceChange DESTROYS accumulated unclaimed fees by resetting offset without crediting
userTokens array grows with duplicates on every buy → gas griefing
No onBalanceChange on sells, transfers, withdrawals, or deposits → fee misallocation
Withdraw-deposit cycle exploits stale offsets for fee theft
Anyone can call onBalanceChange and addFees due to broken Security.sol modifiers

path: Caller invokes onlyOwner or onlyManager protected function
Security.sol modifier evaluates comparison expression without require/revert
Function body executes regardless of caller identity
transformations: No transformation — access control is completely bypassed
sinks: Any state protected by onlyOwner or onlyManager can be modified by anyone
data_flows.trust_changes: CRITICAL: Trust model is completely broken — no privileged operations exist
exposure_risks: Owner/manager can be changed by anyone
Fee parameters can be changed by anyone
feeRedistributor and curvesERC20Factory can be changed by anyone
setCurves in FeeSplitter has no modifier at all
addFees and onBalanceChange callable by anyone
manipulation_risks: Attacker takes ownership, changes all fee destinations to themselves
Attacker sets malicious FeeSplitter to steal fees
Attacker sets malicious ERC20Factory to deploy trojan tokens
Attacker calls setCurves on FeeSplitter pointing to malicious contract, then manipulates fee claims
Attacker calls onBalanceChange to reset victims' fee offsets, stealing their accumulated fees

data_flows.source: Token subject calls buyCurvesTokenForPresale, whitelisted users call buyCurvesTokenWhitelisted
path: Subject sets startTime > block.timestamp, merkleRoot, maxBuy
Whitelisted users buy during presale (block.timestamp < startTime)
Public users buy after startTime via buyCurvesToken
data_flows.trust_changes: Merkle root trusted from subject; MerkleProof.verify validates inclusion
exposure_risks: setWhitelist can be called when supply <= 1 — subject can change whitelist after presale starts
manipulation_risks: Subject can set merkleRoot to 0x0 which may allow any proof to pass depending on MerkleProof implementation
Subject can set maxBuy to 0 which becomes type(uint256).max — effectively unlimited buys

path: Validate not transferring to Curves contract
_transfer: validate balance, update balances, add to ownedCurvesTokenSubjects
Emit Transfer event
data_flows.trust_changes: Token ownership moves from sender to receiver
exposure_risks: transferAllCurvesTokens iterates ownedCurvesTokenSubjects — gas griefing if array is large
ownedCurvesTokenSubjects never removes entries — unbounded array growth
manipulation_risks: CRITICAL: No FeeSplitter notification on transfer — sender continues to accrue fees on transferred tokens until next onBalanceChange, receiver doesn't get fees until next onBalanceChange
Sender can transfer tokens, claim accumulated fees based on old balance, then tokens are effectively double-counted in fee distribution

core_rules: Bonding curve: price = sum of squares formula, first token is free (price=0 at supply=0, amount=1)
First token must be bought by the subject (supply=0 requires msg.sender==subject)
Last token cannot be sold (supply must be > amount after sell)
Internal tokens use 0 decimals, ERC20 uses 18 decimals (1:1e18 mapping)
Fees split between protocol, subject, referral, holders with configurable percentages
ERC20 tokens are backed 1:1 by internal tokens locked in Curves contract
Presale period: only whitelisted users can buy before startTime, public after
economic_incentives: Token holders earn proportional share of holderFee from every trade
Subject earns subjectFee from every trade of their token
Protocol earns protocolFee from buys only (sell protocol fees stuck in contract)
Referrals earn referralFee if set, otherwise fee is absorbed by protocol on buys or lost on sells
Bonding curve creates increasing buy cost and decreasing sell value — early buyers benefit
governance_mechanisms: Owner can set fee parameters, fee redistributor, ERC20 factory
Managers can set max fee percent and external fee percents
Token subjects can set referral destination, name/symbol, whitelist, deploy ERC20
BROKEN: All owner/manager restrictions are non-functional due to Security.sol bug

edge_cases.scenario: Security.sol onlyOwner and onlyManager modifiers do not revert — they evaluate a boolean expression as a statement with no effect
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Only owner/manager should be able to call protected functions
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Anyone can call all protected functions including setManager, transferOwnership, setProtocolFeePercent, setFeeRedistributor, setERC20Factory, addFees, onBalanceChange
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Attacker calls transferOwnership to become owner, changes all fee destinations, sets malicious FeeSplitter/Factory, calls onBalanceChange to reset victims' fee offsets stealing their fees, calls setCurves to point to malicious contract

edge_cases.scenario: FeeSplitter.setCurves() has no access control modifier at all
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Only authorized caller should set the curves contract
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Anyone can call setCurves to point FeeSplitter to any address, enabling manipulation of balanceOf/totalSupply reads
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Attacker deploys malicious contract returning arbitrary balances, calls setCurves to point to it, then claims all ETH in FeeSplitter

edge_cases.scenario: onBalanceChange resets userFeeOffset without first crediting accumulated unclaimed fees via updateFeeCredit
edge_cases.expected_behavior: User's accumulated fees should be preserved when their offset is updated
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Accumulated fees since last onBalanceChange are permanently lost; buying more tokens erases unclaimed holder fees
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Attacker (or anyone via broken access control) calls onBalanceChange for a victim, resetting their offset and destroying their accumulated fees

edge_cases.scenario: Withdraw/deposit cycle exploits stale fee offsets for fee theft
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Fee distribution should accurately reflect token holding periods
edge_cases.actual_behavior: User can withdraw tokens (lowering effective supply for fee calc), wait for fees to be distributed at higher per-token rate, deposit back, then claim at full balance retroactively capturing the higher-rate fees
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: 1) Hold N tokens with offset at C0. 2) Withdraw N tokens (no FeeSplitter update). 3) Wait for holderFees to be distributed — goes to fewer holders at higher rate, cumulativeFeePerToken increases by more per ETH. 4) Deposit N tokens back (no FeeSplitter update). 5) Claim fees — owed calculated as (C_current - C0) * N, capturing fees from period when user held 0 internal tokens.

edge_cases.scenario: No FeeSplitter notification on internal transfers (transferCurvesToken, transferAllCurvesTokens)
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Fee offsets should be updated for both sender and receiver on transfer
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Sender's offset remains stale — can continue claiming fees on tokens they no longer hold. Receiver's offset is stale — may not receive fees for newly received tokens, or may receive backdated fees.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Transfer tokens to another address controlled by attacker, claim fees from both addresses before offsets get updated via future buy

edge_cases.scenario: protocolFee on sells is never sent to protocolFeeDestination — stays locked in Curves contract
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Protocol should receive protocolFee on both buys and sells
edge_cases.actual_behavior: For sells, sellValue = price - all fees sent to seller, subjectFee sent to subject, referralFee sent to referral (if defined), holderFee to FeeSplitter — but protocolFee is deducted from seller payout without being sent anywhere
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Protocol loses revenue from all sell transactions; accumulated stuck ETH grows over time; no mechanism to extract it

edge_cases.scenario: Referral fee on sells lost when no referral defined
edge_cases.expected_behavior: If no referral, the referral fee portion should go to protocol or back to seller
edge_cases.actual_behavior: On sells: sellValue deducts referralFee, but if no referral defined, the referralFee ETH stays in the contract (referral .call is skipped with (true, bytes('')))
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Sellers lose the referral fee portion of their proceeds when no referral is set for the token

edge_cases.scenario: Excess ETH from buy overpayment is never refunded
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Excess msg.value should be refunded to buyer
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Only checks msg.value >= price + totalFee — any excess stays in Curves contract permanently
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Users or contracts that send more than exact price+fees lose the difference; especially dangerous for contracts that estimate gas

edge_cases.scenario: getPrice(supply=0, amount>1) causes arithmetic underflow revert
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Should calculate price for buying multiple tokens starting from supply 0
edge_cases.actual_behavior: The sum2 calculation contains 'supply - 1 + amount' which underflows when supply=0 and amount>1 in Solidity 0.8.7 checked arithmetic: (0 - 1) reverts before adding amount
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Subject is forced to buy exactly 1 token initially; cannot buy multiple tokens when creating curve. This is a design constraint but could be an unintended limitation.

edge_cases.scenario: withdraw() can deploy ERC20 for any subject, bypassing onlyTokenSubject check on _mint
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Only the token subject should control ERC20 deployment and naming
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Any holder of a curves token can call withdraw() which calls _deployERC20 directly (not through _mint which has onlyTokenSubject), deploying the ERC20 with default name/symbol or whatever was pre-set
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Attacker buys 1 token for a subject, immediately withdraws — ERC20 deploys with default name, locking out subject from choosing custom name/symbol

edge_cases.scenario: userTokens array in FeeSplitter accumulates duplicates
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Each token should appear once in userTokens
edge_cases.actual_behavior: onBalanceChange pushes token to userTokens every time balance > 0, without checking for duplicates. Every buy adds a duplicate entry.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Array grows unbounded, increasing gas costs for getUserTokensAndClaimable and batchClaiming. Buying 0 tokens repeatedly costs minimal ETH but grows arrays arbitrarily.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Buying 0 tokens should be rejected as it has no economic purpose
edge_cases.actual_behavior: getPrice(supply, 0) returns 0 for supply > 0. _buyCurvesToken succeeds with 0 ETH, calling _transferFees with price=0, triggering onBalanceChange which pushes duplicate to userTokens, adds to ownedCurvesTokenSubjects
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Gas griefing: attacker calls buyCurvesToken with amount=0 repeatedly to grow userTokens and ownedCurvesTokenSubjects arrays for victim addresses (via cross-effects if applicable)

edge_cases.scenario: FeeSplitter.addFees precision loss when msg.value is small relative to totalSupply
edge_cases.expected_behavior: All fees should be accurately tracked
edge_cases.actual_behavior: cumulativeFeePerToken += (msg.value * PRECISION) / totalSupply_. Since totalSupply_ = (supply - locked) * PRECISION, this simplifies to msg.value / (supply - locked). For msg.value < (supply - locked), the increment rounds to 0 — fees are lost.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Small fee amounts from low-value trades are permanently lost. Not directly exploitable but results in accounting dust.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Total fees should never exceed 100% of price to prevent sell underflow
edge_cases.actual_behavior: setMaxFeePercent only checks existing fees <= new max. If max >= 1 ether (100%), fees could equal or exceed price, causing sellValue underflow and making sells impossible.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: With broken access control, anyone can set maxFeePercent >= 1 ether, then set fee percents to sum > price, breaking all sells (underflow revert) and trapping user funds

edge_cases.scenario: ownedCurvesTokenSubjects array never removes entries even when balance reaches 0
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Sold-out positions should be removed from tracking
edge_cases.actual_behavior: After selling all tokens, the subject remains in ownedCurvesTokenSubjects. transferAllCurvesTokens iterates entire array including zero-balance entries.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Gas griefing for transferAllCurvesTokens — unbounded iteration over stale entries

edge_cases.expected_behavior: All users including smart contract wallets should be able to claim
edge_cases.actual_behavior: .transfer() limits gas to 2300, which is insufficient for contracts with receive() logic or on chains where gas costs differ. Claims revert for such users.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Smart contract wallets or multisigs cannot claim fees. Not exploitable but causes permanent fee loss for affected users.

edge_cases.scenario: Front-running buy transactions on bonding curve
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Attacker sees pending buy in mempool, front-runs with own buy (increasing price), victim buys at higher price, attacker back-runs with sell at profit
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Classic sandwich attack — bonding curve has no slippage protection or deadline parameter

assumption_violations: CRITICAL: Security.sol assumes `msg.sender == owner;` enforces access control — it's a no-op expression statement, not a require
FeeSplitter assumes onBalanceChange is called for ALL balance changes — it's only called for buyers during buy, not for sellers, transfers, withdrawals, or deposits
FeeSplitter.onBalanceChange assumes setting offset without crediting owed fees is safe — it destroys accumulated fees
Protocol assumes protocolFee is collected on sells — it is deducted from seller but never forwarded
Bonding curve assumes contract ETH balance equals sum of all positions' values — stuck fees break this assumption
withdraw() assumes only subjects deploy ERC20 tokens — any holder can trigger deployment
getPrice assumes supply >= 1 for amounts > 1 — underflows for supply=0, amount>1
Fee calculation assumes sum of individual fee percentages / 1 ether < 1 — no hard cap enforcement
game_theory_risks: Fee theft via withdraw-deposit cycle: rational actors can extract disproportionate holder fees by temporarily reducing their internal balance during fee distribution periods
Sandwich attacks on bonding curve buys with no slippage protection
With broken access control, any actor can become owner and extract all protocol value
Subject can front-run their own token by setting fees to maximum before anticipated high-value trades
Rational subjects have no referral set, causing referral fee on sells to be lost — misaligned with user expectations
Early token holders can withdraw to ERC20, reducing FeeSplitter totalSupply, concentrating all future holder fees to remaining internal holders
integration_risks: CurvesERC20 is owned by Curves contract — if Curves address changes or is compromised, ERC20 mint/burn becomes exploitable
FeeSplitter reads live balance from Curves — any desynchronization (e.g., via broken setCurves) allows fee manipulation
OpenZeppelin MerkleProof dependency — standard and safe, but merkleRoot of bytes32(0) with empty proof returns true for leaf hash matching 0x0
External .call for ETH transfers can fail for contracts that reject ETH or require more gas — breaking buy/sell flows
calculation_errors: getPrice: sum2 underflows when supply=0 and amount>1 due to unchecked supply-1 in Solidity 0.8.7
sell _transferFees: sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee deducts protocolFee from seller but never sends it to protocol
FeeSplitter.addFees: cumulativeFeePerToken increment = msg.value/(supply-locked) loses precision via integer division for small fees
FeeSplitter.onBalanceChange: missing updateFeeCredit call means owed = (cumulativeFeePerToken - oldOffset) * balance is discarded when offset is reset

token_semantics.notes: Standard ERC20 with mint/burn restricted to owner (Curves contract). No hooks or callbacks. Ownable via OpenZeppelin. Each CurvesERC20 instance is deployed per curvesTokenSubject via CurvesERC20Factory.
Internal accounting in Curves.sol via curvesTokenBalance mapping. No ERC20 interface. Whole units only. 1 internal token = 1e18 ERC20 tokens on withdraw.
Native ETH used for bonding curve purchases, fee distribution, and seller payouts. Transferred via .call{value:} in Curves and .transfer() in FeeSplitter.

invariants.name: supply_conservation
invariants.notes: Total supply should equal sum of all holder balances including address(this). Maintained by _buyCurvesToken, sellCurvesToken, _transfer.

invariants.name: erc20_backing
invariants.notes: ERC20 total supply should equal internal tokens locked in Curves contract * 1e18. Maintained by withdraw (mint) and deposit (burn).

invariants.name: eth_solvency_intended
invariants.notes: VIOLATED: Contract accumulates extra ETH from (1) stuck protocolFee on sells, (2) stuck referralFee on sells without referral, (3) excess ETH from buy overpayments. Contract balance > theoretical bonding curve backing.

invariants.name: fee_offset_consistency
invariants.notes: VIOLATED: onBalanceChange resets offset without crediting owed fees. Transfers/withdrawals/deposits don't update offsets at all. Fee distribution is inaccurate.

invariants.name: access_control_integrity
invariants.notes: VIOLATED: Security.sol modifiers are no-ops. Any address can modify owner and managers state.
